name: Jaguar
tagline: The jungle's spotted shadow — a stealthy giant that strikes from the trees.
intro: Reports of jaguars trailing people through the jungle have been recorded for centuries. Imagine hearing a rustle of underbrush and turning to see a pair of fierce yellow eyes fixed on you! But instead of attacking the people, all these jaguars did was frighten them, and then vanish. Experts think that when the big cats act like this, they may be simply escorting the human intruders out of the jaguars' territory.

facts.label: How it kills
facts.value: Kills by biting through skull of victim

facts.label: Favorite meals
facts.value: Mostly mammals plus fish, birds, turtles, and their eggs

facts.label: Watch out
facts.value: To find a jaguar, look for a tropical stream or river. This shy cat loves to swim and fish. At up to 153 kilograms (340lbs), it makes quite a splash!

topics.title: Spots before your eyes
topics.text: Can you tell these spotted cats apart? Look closely at their fur. Although they're all spotted, their spots are all different.

captions.label: Cheetah fur
captions.text: Close-up of the cheetah's small, solid black spots.

captions.label: Jaguar fur
captions.text: Close-up of the jaguar's rosette pattern with spots inside each ring.

captions.label: Ocelot fur
captions.text: Close-up of the ocelot's irregular blotched and streaked pattern.

captions.label: Leopard fur
captions.text: Close-up of the leopard's open rosettes without inner spots.

topics.title: Hiding out
topics.text: Ever notice the patterns of light and shade in a forest? That's why it's hard to see spotted forest cats–an alternating pattern of light and dark patches breaks up the shapes of the animals, so that they blend into the environment. This jaguar is neatly camouflaged. As long as it stays still, another animal might not even notice it.

topics.title: Super jock
topics.text: Like all hunting cats, jaguars are great climbers and jumpers. They like to lie in ambush in the bushes. Another favorite jaguar hunting technique is to scale tall trees that overhang paths and then leap down onto animals passing below. Imagine seeing this cat jumping down on you!

topics.title: On the menu
topics.text: A jaguar needs a lot of meat to power its muscular body. It will eat many types of animals (including the ones shown here), and it's famous for its hunting techiques.

captions.label: Caiman
captions.text: A crocodilian reptile hunted by jaguars near tropical waterways.

captions.label: Amazonian catfish
captions.text: A large freshwater fish the jaguar snatches from rivers.

captions.label: Tapir
captions.text: A large hoofed mammal and one of the jaguar's biggest prey.

topics.title: Another jungle cat
topics.text: A jaguar might meet an ocelot–they both roam through the forests and scrublands of Central America. But the ocelot would rather avoid this meeting with its cousin. An average ocelot weighs only a fraction of what a jaguar does, but it's still enough to make a good size meal for its bigger cousin!

topics.title: Art object
topics.text: Cultures throughout history have honored big cats as symbols of strength and intelligence. Cats appear in paintings, statues, and carvings all over the world.

captions.label: Jaguar worship
captions.text: Both the Aztecs and the Mayas, ancient rulers of Mexico and Central America, revered jaguars. This Mayan statue from Chichén Itzá is inlaid with precious jade. The bowl on its back is for offerings.

_provenance.note: Text liberated from original screen art; images & clip restored from disc. Original media is Microsoft/supplier copyright — placeholder pending swap to open-licensed assets.

video.title: Loner
video.caption: Jaguars spend their days alone, prowling through the forests. Felines of all sizes are exceptional hunters: that's why they can survive on their own. Except for lions, which live and hunt in groups, and for mothers accompanied by their young, most cats lead solitary lives.
